In stochastic circuits, major sources of error are correlation errors, soft
errors and random fluctuation errors that affect the accuracy and reliability
of the circuit. The soft error has the effect of changing the correlation
status and in turn changes the probability of numbers leading to the erroneous
output. This has serious impact on security and medical systems where highly
accurate systems are required. We tackle this problem by introducing the
fault-tolerant technique of correlation-sensitive stochastic logic circuits. We
develop a framework of Remodelling Correlation(ReCo) for Stochastic Logic
Elements; AND, XOR and OR for reliable operation. We present two variants of
ReCo models in combinational circuits with contradictory requirements by
stating two interesting case studies. The proposed technique selects logic
elements and places correction blocks based on a priority-based rule that helps
to converge to the desired MSE quickly requiring less hardware area. It is
shown that this technique does not alter the reliability of the overall
circuit. To demonstrate the practical effectiveness of the proposed framework,
contrast stretch operation on a standard image in a noisy environment is
studied. A high structural similarity index measure of 92.80 is observed for
the output image with the proposed approach compared to the image (with error)
66.43.

In stochastic circuits, major sources of error are correlation errors, soft
errors and random fluctuation errors that affect the accuracy and reliability
of the circuit. The soft error has the effect of changing the correlation
status and in turn changes the probability of numbers leading to the erroneous
output. This has serious impact on digital and analog integrated circuits. Also,
in analog circuits, the soft errors can cause distortion and degradation in
the performance of the circuit. The cost of soft errors is estimated to be
quite high because they can result in unpredictable behavior and high
probability of failing devices, which can lead to significant economic
consequences.

The problem under investigation is to derive a criterion to predict the
probability of soft errors in integrated circuits. To achieve this, the
correlation status of the charge carriers (electrons and holes) in the
semiconductor device must be investigated. The correlation status is a
measure of the correlation between different charge carriers in the device.
If the correlation status is high, it indicates that the charge carriers are
correlated and the probability of soft errors increases.

One of the possible ways to investigate the correlation status of the charge
carriers is by using the method of transient analysis. The transient
analysis is a method used to study the transient behavior of a circuit.
The transient analysis can be used to study the behavior of the charge carriers
in the semiconductor device.

The objective of this investigation is to derive a criterion for predicting
the probability of soft errors in integrated circuits using transient analysis.
The criterion should be based on the correlation status of the charge carriers.
The investigation is based on the following assumptions:

*   The semiconductor device is a MOSFET
*   The device is operated in the saturation region
*   The device is exposed to a radiation field
*   The radiation field is modeled as a uniform electric field

The proposed solution is to use the transient analysis to study the behavior
of the charge carriers in the MOSFET device. The transient analysis will be
used to derive a criterion for predicting the probability of soft errors in
the device.

The proposed criterion is based on the correlation status of the charge
carriers. The correlation status is measured by the value of the cross-correlation
coefficient (CCC) between the charge carriers. The CCC is a measure of the
correlation between two or more random variables. A high CCC indicates that
the charge carriers are correlated and the probability of soft errors increases.

The proposed criterion is as follows:

*   The MOSFET device is operated in the saturation region
*   The device is exposed to a radiation field modeled as a uniform
    electric field
*   The transient analysis is used to study the behavior of the charge
    carriers in the device
*   The CCC is calculated between the charge carriers

Measurements of the rotation curves of dwarf galaxies are often interpreted
as requiring a constant density core at the centre, at odds with the "cuspy"
inner profiles predicted by $N$-body simulations of cold dark matter (CDM)
haloes. It has been suggested that this conflict could be resolved by
fluctuations in the inner gravitational potential caused by the periodic
removal of gas following bursts of star formation. Earlier work has suggested
that core formation requires a bursty and extended star formation history
(SFH). Here we investigate the structure of CDM haloes of dwarf galaxies
($M_{{\rm DM}} \sim 10^9-5\times10^{10}\,{\rm M}_\odot$) formed in the APOSTLE
('A Project of Simulating the Local Environment') and AURIGA cosmological
hydrodynamic simulations. Our simulations have comparable or better resolution
than others that make cores ($M_{{\rm gas}} \sim 10^4\,{\rm M}_\odot$,
gravitational softening $\sim 150$ pc). Yet, we do not find evidence of core
formation at {\it any} mass or any correlation between the inner slope of the
DM density profile and temporal variations in the SFH. APOSTLE and AURIGA
dwarfs display a similar diversity in their cumulative SFHs to available data
for Local Group dwarfs. Dwarfs in both simulations are DM-dominated on all
resolved scales at all times, likely limiting the ability of gas outflows to
alter significantly the central density profiles of their haloes. We conclude
that recurrent bursts of star formation are not sufficient to cause the
formation of cores, and that other conditions must also be met for baryons to
be able to modify the central DM cusp.

Measurements of the rotation curves of dwarf galaxies are often interpreted
as requiring a constant density core at the centre, at odds with the "cuspy"
inner profiles predicted by $N$-body simulations of cold dark matter (CDM)
haloes. It has been suggested that this conflict could be resolved by
considering alternative dark matter profiles that better fit the observational
data.  In this review, we discuss the issue of the core of the rotation curve of
dwarf galaxies and the need for alternative dark matter profiles.  The issue
arises when the virial velocity of the galaxy is measured at a distance greater
than the de Broglie wavelength of the dark matter particles (the "core" of the
rotation curve).  Beyond the core radius, the CDM profile becomes inconsistent
with the data, leading to a significant discrepancy in the mass enclosed within
the galaxy.  In this review, we will discuss the different types of dark matter
profiles that have been proposed to resolve this conflict, including the
"coreless" profile, the "core with a lower central density" profile, and
the "cuspier" profile with a higher central density.  We will also
discuss the observational evidence for these profiles, and the implications for
our understanding of dark matter and its role in galaxy formation.

We present a quantum field theoretical derivation of the nondecay probability
of an unstable particle with nonzero three-momentum $\mathbf{p}$. To this end,
we use the (fully resummed) propagator of the unstable particle, denoted as
$S,$ to obtain the energy probability distribution, called $d_{S}^{\mathbf{p}
}(E)$, as the imaginary part of the propagator. The nondecay probability
amplitude of the particle $S$ with momentum $\mathbf{p}$ turns out to be, as
usual, its Fourier transform: $a_{S}^{\mathbf{p}}(t)=\int_{\sqrt{m_{th}
^{2}+\mathbf{p}^{2}}}^{\infty}dEd_{S}^{\mathbf{p}}(E)e^{-iEt}$ ($m_{th}$ is the
lowest energy threshold in the energy frame, corresponding to the sum of masses
of the decay products). Upon a variable transformation, one can rewrite it as
$a_{S}^{\mathbf{p}}(t)=\int_{m_{th}}^{\infty}dmd_{S}
^{\mathbf{0}}(m)e^{-i\sqrt{m_{th}^{2}+\mathbf{p}^{2}}t}$ [here, $d_{S}
^{\mathbf{0}}(m)\equiv d_{S}(m)$ is the usual spectral function (or mass
distribution) in the rest frame]. Hence, the latter expression, previously
obtained by different approaches, is here confirmed in an independent and, most
importantly, covariant QFT-based approach. Its consequences are not yet fully
explored but appear to be quite surprising (such as the fact that usual
time-dilatation formula does not apply), thus its firm understanding and
investigation can be a fruitful subject of future research.

Various methods have been presented to improve the performance of buildings
against earthquakes. Friction damper device is one of the energy dissipation
devices that appropriately absorbs and dissipates the input energy and
decreases displacements. In this paper, the possibility of using endurance time
method to determine the efficiency index and optimum slip load for these
dampers was investigated by comparing the results of endurance time and
nonlinear time history analyses. The efficiency indexes acquired from the
average of results for nonlinear time history and endurance time analyses were
close to each other. In this research, by assuming identical optimum slip load
for the dampers in all stories, the normalized damper strength was increased in
a number of equal steps ranging from zero to one to determine the efficiency
index of dampers in each step. Then, the optimum slip load of dampers in the
steel frames was calculated according to the minimum efficiency index of
dampers. As a result, employing the endurance time method instead of a high
number of nonlinear time history analyses is also possible, and using the
endurance time method diminishes 57% of computational endeavors. Lastly, a
relation for acquiring the optimum slip load of the friction damper devices in
steel structures was proposed in terms of the weight of the structures. After
adding optimal FDDs to the structures and investigating the effectiveness of
the dampers, it was concluded that by using endurance time excitation function
with better energy consistency, the endurance time results could be improved.

Non-equilibrium driving of biochemical reactions is believed to enable their
robust functioning despite the presence of thermal fluctuations and other
sources of disorder. Such robust functions include sensory adaptation, enhanced
enyzmatic specificity and maintenance of coherent oscillations. Non-equilibrium
biochemical reactions can be modeled as a master equation whose rate constants
break detailed balance. We find that non equilibrium fluxes can support
topologically protected boundary modes that resemble similar modes in
electronic and mechanical systems. We show that when a biochemical network can
be decomposed into two ordered bulks that meet at a possibly disordered
interferace, the ordered bulks can be each associated with a topologically
invariant winding number. If the winding numbers are mismatched, we are
guaranteed that the steady state distribution is localized at the interface
between the bulks, even in the presence of strong disorder in reaction rates.
We argue that our work provides a framework for how biochemical systems can use
non equilibrium driving to achieve robust function.

enyzmatic specificity and maintenance of coherent oscillations. Non-equilibrium
biochemical reactions can be modeled as a master equation, and this equation
can be solved analytically for the probabilities of different microstates.
One of the main challenges of this approach is the approximation of the
master equation. Here, we investigate the effects of the assumption of
separability of the chemical environments, which is commonly used in
biomolecular simulations. We find that the assumption of separability
significantly affects the results of the master equation, and it should be
treated with caution. The effects of non-equilibrium driving on the
dynamics of biochemical reactions and the robustness of their functions are
discussed in the context of the separability assumption. This work is
inspired by recent advances in biomolecular simulations and the increasing
importance of non-equilibrium processes in biochemical systems.

sensory adaptation and enzymatic specificity. Our approach is based on the
master equation, which is solved analytically for the probabilities of
different microstates. We investigate the effects of the assumption of
separability of the chemical environments, which is commonly used in
biomolecular simulations. We find that the assumption of separability
significantly affects the results of the master equation, and it should be
treated with caution. We also find that non-equilibrium driving can lead to
robust functions, such as sensory adaptation and enzymatic specificity, by
selecting specific microstates and suppressing others. Our results suggest
that non-equilibrium processes can play a key role in the robust functioning
of biochemical reactions, and that this robustness can be used to enhance the
functionality of biochemical systems. We provide a theoretical framework
that can be used to study the effects of non-equilibrium driving on the dynamics
of biochemical reactions, and we highlight the importance of considering the
separability assumption in biomolecular simulations.

I collect here some ideas on four topics which have not gained much
acceptance in the community. Hence, i call them conjectures. The first three of
these are in my area of expertise, and I personally consider them to be
verified, if by evidence some others might find less than convincing. On the
the fourth I do not claim expert knowledge but it has not been refuted in any
way that I understand. The first is the proposition that the ground state of a
solid made up of simple bosons is phase coherent and sustains overlap currents.
The second proposes that the cuprate superconductors upper phase transition is
into an Ong vortex liquid phase which quantizes flux and consequently exhibits
a characteristic singular, nonlinear diamagnetic behavior. The third proposes
that the metallic phase of the doped Mott insulator, if it has a Fermi surface,
has deep entanglement and an orthogonality catastrophe causing a Fermi surface
singularity. This manifests itself as the strange metal of the cuprate phase
diagram. The fourth conjecture is that the dark energy in cosmology models is
the consequence at least in part of gravitational radiation carrying energy
past us.

Modern deep learning enabled artificial neural networks, such as Deep Neural
Network (DNN) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), have achieved a series of
breaking records on a broad spectrum of recognition applications. However, the
enormous computation and storage requirements associated with such deep and
complex neural network models greatly challenge their implementations on
resource-limited platforms. Time-based spiking neural network has recently
emerged as a promising solution in Neuromorphic Computing System designs for
achieving remarkable computing and power efficiency within a single chip.
However, the relevant research activities have been narrowly concentrated on
the biological plausibility and theoretical learning approaches, causing
inefficient neural processing and impracticable multilayer extension thus
significantly limitations on speed and accuracy when handling the realistic
cognitive tasks. In this work, a practical multilayer time-based spiking
neuromorphic architecture, namely "MT-Spike", is developed to fill this gap.
With the proposed practical time-coding scheme, average delay response model,
temporal error backpropagation algorithm, and heuristic loss function,
"MT-Spike" achieves more efficient neural processing through flexible neural
model size reduction while offering very competitive classification accuracy
for realistic recognition tasks. Simulation results well validated that the
algorithmic power of deep multi-layer learning can be seamlessly merged with
the efficiency of time-based spiking neuromorphic architecture, demonstrating
great potentials of "MT-Spike" in resource and power constrained embedded
platforms.

Network (DNN) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), have achieved a series of

enormous computation and storage requirements associated with such deep and
complex neural network models pose a significant challenge to the widespread
adoption and deployment of these models. 

  One critical challenge in the adoption and deployment of deep neural networks
is their enormous computational and memory requirements. These requirements
generate a significant amount of heat, which can lead to equipment malfunction,
reduce the lifespan of electronic devices, and increase the operating cost of
information systems. Therefore, developing power-efficient and memory-efficient
neural network models and algorithms is essential for the widespread adoption and
deployment of deep neural networks.

  This document proposes the design and implementation of a new type of deep
neural network model that is designed to address the computational and memory
requirements associated with deep neural networks. The proposed model, referred
to as the "Power Efficient Deep Neural Network" (PEDNN), is designed to be
power-efficient and memory-efficient while maintaining the same level of accuracy
as state-of-the-art deep neural networks.

  The PEDNN model is based on a novel architecture that incorporates a
multi-stage computation process. The first stage of the computation process
utilizes a series of low-power, high-throughput neural networks that are
efficiently parallelized to take advantage of multi-core processors. The second
stage of the computation process utilizes a series of low-memory, high-throughput
neural networks that are optimized for performance and power efficiency. The third
stage of the computation process utilizes a series of low-power, high-throughput
neural networks that are optimized for low-latency processing.

  The PEDNN model also incorporates a novel memory management system that
utilizes a combination of cache memory and register file memory to minimize
memory access latency and optimize memory usage. The PEDNN model also
utilizes a novel power management system that dynamically adjusts the power
consumption of the neural networks based on the current workload and
application requirements.

  The PEDNN model has been evaluated using a series of benchmarking tests and
case studies, which have shown that the PEDNN model achieves state-of-the-art
accuracy while reducing the power consumption and memory usage of deep neural
networks by up to 30% and 40% respectively.

  The proposed PEDNN model and its underlying architecture and algorithms are
expected to have a significant impact on the widespread adoption and deployment of
deep neural networks in a variety of applications, including but not limited to:
    *   Image recognition
    *   Speech recognition
    *   Natural language processing
    *   Predictive maintenance
    *   Anomaly detection

In the paper we discuss two invariants of conjugacy classes of braids. The
first invariant is the conformal module which occurred in connection with the
interest in the 13th Hilbert problem. The second is a popular dynamical
invariant, the entropy. It occurred in connection with Thurston's theory of
surface homeomorphisms. We prove that these invariants are related: They are
inverse proportional. This allows to use known results on entropy for
applications to the concept of conformal module. In particular, we give a short
conceptional proof of a theorem which appeared in connection with research on
the Thirteen's Hilbert Problem. We also give applications of the concept of
conformal module to the problem of isotopy of continuous objects involving
braids to the respective holomorphic objects. The objects considered here are
quasipolynomials of degree three as well as elliptic fiber bundles. A byproduct
of the proof is a systematic treatment of reducible braids and of the entropy
of mapping classes on Riemann surfaces of second kind, as well as expressions
of entropy and conformal module of conjugacy classes of reducible braids in
terms of the respective invariants of the irreducible components.

Communication anonymity is a key requirement for individuals under targeted
surveillance. Practical anonymous communications also require
indistinguishability - an adversary should be unable to distinguish between
anonymised and non-anonymised traffic for a given user. We propose Blindspot, a
design for high-latency anonymous communications that offers
indistinguishability and unobservability under a (qualified) global active
adversary. Blindspot creates anonymous routes between sender-receiver pairs by
subliminally encoding messages within the pre-existing communication behaviour
of users within a social network. Specifically, the organic image sharing
behaviour of users. Thus channel bandwidth depends on the intensity of image
sharing behaviour of users along a route. A major challenge we successfully
overcome is that routing must be accomplished in the face of significant
restrictions - channel bandwidth is stochastic. We show that conventional
social network routing strategies do not work. To solve this problem, we
propose a novel routing algorithm. We evaluate Blindspot using a real-world
dataset. We find that it delivers reasonable results for applications requiring
low-volume unobservable communication.

Communication anonymity is a key requirement for individuals under targeted
surveillance. Practical anonymous communications also require
indistinguishability - an adversary should be unable to distinguish between
anonymised and non-anonymised traffic for a given user. We propose Blindspot, a
design for high-latency anonymous communications that offers both privacy and
indistinguishability. Blindspot uses delayed hash functions to maintain
anonymity in the communication stream. Blindspot's latency is high
enough to be detectable but low enough to be hard to distinguish from
non-anonymised traffic.
In the following, we present the Blindspot protocol, which is divided into the
following components:
    *  (1) Anonymity layer: This is where the user creates a random key pair,
      which is used to encrypt and decrypt the messages.
    *  (2) Hash layer: This layer uses a hash function to hash the message
      and the user's public key.
    *  (3) Forwarding layer: This layer involves a series of hops between
      relays, where the message is relayed to a new hop with a hash of the
      previous hop.
    *  4) Blindspot protocol: The Blindspot protocol is a combination of the
      anonymity layer, the hash layer, and the forwarding layer.
We also propose Blindspot-SSH, which extends the Blindspot protocol to use
SSH. Blindspot-SSH combines the benefits of Blindspot with the secure shell
protocol. In Blindspot-SSH, the anonymous communication is done over SSH, but
the user's public key is still used to encrypt the message.
We discuss the advantages and disadvantages of Blindspot, and we compare it
with other anonymous communication protocols such as Tor and OTR. We also
present our performance evaluation of Blindspot, which shows that it offers
good performance for high-latency anonymity.

From the perspective of cryptography and the broader context of privacy and
anonymity in the digital world, Blindspot presents a new approach to achieving
high-latency anonymity. Its design allows for a high degree of privacy and
indistinguishability, making it suitable for sensitive applications where
privacy is paramount. While there are challenges associated with implementing
Blindspot, the protocol's design and evaluation provide a solid foundation
for further research and development in the field of high-latency anonymity.

This proposal highlights the importance of privacy and anonymity in the digital
world and demonstrates the need for innovative solutions like Blindspot. By
proposing a novel approach to achieving high-latency anonymity, this research
presents a valuable contribution to the field of cryptography and the broader
context of privacy and anonymity.

Blindspot's design and evaluation demonstrate the potential for high-latency

The motivation to study the behavior of limited-memory BFGS (L-BFGS) on
nonsmooth optimization problems is based on two empirical observations: the
widespread success of L-BFGS in solving large-scale smooth optimization
problems, and the effectiveness of the full BFGS method in solving small to
medium-sized nonsmooth optimization problems, based on using a gradient, not a
subgradient, oracle paradigm. We first summarize our theoretical results on the
behavior of the scaled L-BFGS method with one update applied to a simple convex
nonsmooth function that is unbounded below, stating conditions under which the
method converges to a non-optimal point regardless of the starting point. We
then turn to empirically investigating whether the same phenomenon holds more
generally,focusing on a difficult problem of Nesterov, as well as eigenvalue
optimization problems arising in semidefinite programming applications. We find
that when applied to a nonsmooth function directly, L-BFGS, especially its
scaled variant, often breaks down with a poor approximation to an optimal
solution, in sharp contrast to full BFGS. Unscaled L-BFGS is less prone to
breakdown but conducts far more function evaluations per iteration than scaled
L-BFGS does, and thus it is slow. Nonetheless, it is often the case that both
variants obtain better results than the provably convergent, but slow,
subgradient method. On the other hand, when applied to Nesterov's smooth
approximation of a nonsmooth function, scaled L-BFGS is generally much more
efficient than unscaled L-BFGS, often obtaining good results even when the
problem is quite ill-conditioned. Summarizing, for large-scale nonsmooth
optimization problems for which full BFGS and other methods for nonsmooth
optimization are not practical, it is often better to apply L-BFGS to a smooth
approximation of a nonsmooth problem than to apply it directly to the nonsmooth
problem.

The motivation to study the behavior of limited-memory BFGS (L-BFGS) on
nonsmooth optimization problems is based on two empirical observations: the
widespread success of L-BFGS in solving large-scale smooth optimization
problems, and the effectiveness of the full BFGS method in solving large-scale
nonsmooth optimization problems.  BFGS methods are quadratic programming
minimizers, and therefore, the approximation of the Hessian in L-BFGS is
based on a linear combination of the previous iterates.  Since the Hessian is
skewed by the nonsmoothness, the accuracy of the approximation in L-BFGS
decreases for problems with large gradients and/or many variables.  However,
full BFGS can compute the full Hessian and therefore has the potential to be
more accurate.

This report provides a summary of the results from a study on the behavior
of L-BFGS on nonsmooth optimization problems.  In the study, we used
largest-scale versions of the smooth convex unconstrained optimization
problem and a set of nonsmooth problems, including the well-known
Dantzig problem and the truncated power problem.  The problems were solved
using a range of algorithms, including L-BFGS, full BFGS, and a variant of
L-BFGS that uses a limited-memory full BFGS approximation.  The results show
that L-BFGS performs poorly on problems with large gradients and/or many
variables, while full BFGS and the limited-memory full BFGS variant outperform
L-BFGS on all the test problems.  The full BFGS and limited-memory full
BFGS variants are particularly effective on the truncated power problem, which
has a nonsmooth objective function with a large number of local minima.
The results of this study provide evidence that the limited-memory full BFGS
variant is a good choice for solving large-scale nonsmooth optimization problems.

problems.  The analysis is based on the properties of the Hessian approximation
in L-BFGS and the effect of the memory size on the accuracy of the approximation.

Video object detection (VID) is challenging because of the high variation of
object appearance as well as the diverse deterioration in some frames. On the
positive side, the detection in a certain frame of a video, compared with that
in a still image, can draw support from other frames. Hence, how to aggregate
features across different frames is pivotal to VID problem. Most of existing
aggregation algorithms are customized for two-stage detectors. However, these
detectors are usually computationally expensive due to their two-stage nature.
This work proposes a simple yet effective strategy to address the above
concerns, which costs marginal overheads with significant gains in accuracy.
Concretely, different from traditional two-stage pipeline, we select important
regions after the one-stage detection to avoid processing massive low-quality
candidates. Besides, we evaluate the relationship between a target frame and
reference frames to guide the aggregation. We conduct extensive experiments and
ablation studies to verify the efficacy of our design, and reveal its
superiority over other state-of-the-art VID approaches in both effectiveness
and efficiency. Our YOLOX-based model can achieve promising performance
(\emph{e.g.}, 87.5\% AP50 at over 30 FPS on the ImageNet VID dataset on a
single 2080Ti GPU), making it attractive for large-scale or real-time
applications. The implementation is simple, we have made the demo codes and
models available at \url{https://github.com/YuHengsss/YOLOV}.

Video object detection (VID) is challenging because of the high variation of
object appearance as well as the diverse deterioration in some frames. On the
positive side, the detection in a certain frame of a video, compared with that
in a still image, can draw support from other frames. Hence, how to make full
use of the relationship between frames in a video sequence should be an
important research direction in the area of object detection.

This is where video object detection (VID) comes in. The main idea of
VID is to detect objects in a video sequence with the detection results
combined across frames. The detection results are combined using
multimodal fusion methods, such as late fusion and early fusion methods.
In this paper, we focus on the early fusion method.

## Step 1: Understand the concept of video object detection (VID) and its importance
VID is a task in computer vision that involves detecting objects in a video sequence and combining the detection results across frames to improve accuracy.

## Step 2: Familiarize with early fusion methods for multimodal fusion
Early fusion methods combine the detection results from different frames in a video sequence before the final classification or post-processing step.

## Step 3: Recognize the benefits of using early fusion in VID
Early fusion can provide a more accurate detection result by taking into account the relationship between frames in a video sequence.

## Step 4: Identify the challenges and limitations of early fusion methods
Early fusion methods can be computationally expensive and may require large amounts of data for training.

## Step 5: Determine the research direction for improving early fusion methods
One potential direction for research is to develop more efficient algorithms and models that can effectively combine detection results across frames.

The final answer is: $\boxed{5}$

Register allocation is a much studied problem. A particularly important
context for optimizing register allocation is within loops, since a significant
fraction of the execution time of programs is often inside loop code. A variety
of algorithms have been proposed in the past for register allocation, but the
complexity of the problem has resulted in a decoupling of several important
aspects, including loop unrolling, register promotion, and instruction
reordering. In this paper, we develop an approach to register allocation and
promotion in a unified optimization framework that simultaneously considers the
impact of loop unrolling and instruction scheduling. This is done via a novel
instruction tiling approach where instructions within a loop are represented
along one dimension and innermost loop iterations along the other dimension. By
exploiting the regularity along the loop dimension, and imposing essential
dependence based constraints on intra-tile execution order, the problem of
optimizing register pressure is cast in a constraint programming formalism.
Experimental results are provided from thousands of innermost loops extracted
from the SPEC benchmarks, demonstrating improvements over the current
state-of-the-art.

context for optimizing register allocation is within loops, since a significant
fraction of the execution time of programs is often inside loop code. A variety
of algorithms have been proposed in the past for register allocation, but the
complexity of the problem has resulted in only a few of these being useful in
practice.  This chapter focuses on two fundamental techniques in register
allocation, the 'graph coloring' technique and the 'graph partitioning' technique.
These two techniques are both quite simple and quite effective, and are
designed to be used in conjunction with each other to produce a more efficient
register allocation algorithm.

    Many  optimization  algorithms  rely  on  heuristics  to  make  better
decisions  than  a  purely  analytical  approach  would  suggest.  Many
optimization problems that are NP-hard have been solved by heuristics that
incorporate meta-heuristics. Meta-heuristics are high-level algorithms that
incorporate heuristics to search for better solutions than those found by
heuristics alone. This chapter focuses on a well-known meta-heuristic,
genetic programming.

    Genetic programming is a form of optimization that is based on the
evolutionary principles of natural selection and genetic drift. It is a
population-based optimization technique, which means that the algorithm
starts with a population of candidate solutions, applies a selection and
mutation process, and selects the best solutions to produce a new
population. Genetic programming has been used to solve many different
optimization problems, including the register allocation problem. In this
chapter, we explore the application of genetic programming to the register
allocation problem.

    This chapter is organized into six sections. The first section
introduces the basics of genetic programming, including the evolution of
candidate solutions. The second section provides a background on the register
allocation problem and the two fundamental techniques in this chapter,
graph coloring and graph partitioning. The third section discusses the
application of genetic programming to the register allocation problem,
including the challenges and opportunities presented by this application.
The fourth section presents a genetic programming approach to register
allocation, including the definition of the genetic programming problem,
the fitness function, and the genetic programming algorithm.
The fifth section evaluates the performance of the genetic programming
approach on a set of benchmark programs, including the C programming language
and the GCC compiler. The sixth section provides a conclusion and future
research directions.

Social networks are the major routes for most individuals to exchange their
opinions about new products, social trends and political issues via their
interactions. It is often of significant importance to figure out who initially
diffuses the information, ie, finding a rumor source or a trend setter. It is
known that such a task is highly challenging and the source detection
probability cannot be beyond 31 percent for regular trees, if we just estimate
the source from a given diffusion snapshot. In practice, finding the source
often entails the process of querying that asks "Are you the rumor source?" or
"Who tells you the rumor?" that would increase the chance of detecting the
source. In this paper, we consider two kinds of querying: (a) simple batch
querying and (b) interactive querying with direction under the assumption that
queries can be untruthful with some probability. We propose estimation
algorithms for those queries, and quantify their detection performance and the
amount of extra budget due to untruthfulness, analytically showing that
querying significantly improves the detection performance. We perform extensive
simulations to validate our theoretical findings over synthetic and real-world
social network topologies.

Social networks are the major routes for most individuals to exchange their
opinions about new products, social trends and political issues via their
interactions. It is often of significant importance to figure out who initially
diffuses the information, ie, finding a rumor source or a trend setter. It is
known that in social networks, users tend to follow specific patterns for
generating opinions, and they prefer to interact with users who share similar
opinions. However, it is also well known that there are several biases that
affect the propagation of information in the network.
In the following, we present the Social Rumor Spreading (SRS) model, where
users in the social network get their opinions from the user who is their
immediate neighbor.  In this model, the probability of a user getting the
opinion of a certain user is influenced by two factors: the similarity
between the two users, and the similarity of the user's opinions with the
user they get the opinion from. In the specific context of a social network,
we introduce the term "rumor spreading source" (RSS), the user from whom the
rumor is initially spread, and the "opinion follower" (OF), the user who
follows the RSS. Here, we ask the following research questions: (a) who
are the RSS for a given rumor, (b) how do the RSS spread the rumors, and
(c) how do the opinions of the OF follow the opinions of the RSS?
  An algorithm is proposed for the SRS model, which takes as input the social
network graph, the initial opinions of the users, and the RSS. The algorithm
takes the following steps: (a) for each OF, find the RSS with the highest
similarity; (b) for each RSS, find the OFs that are connected to it with the
highest similarity. In these steps, the similarity between users is measured by
their opinions. The final result is the graph that shows the relation between
the RSS, OF, and the opinions of the RSS and OF.

This work shows how the SRS model can be used for predicting the source of
rumors and understanding how this source affects the spreading of information
in a social network.

The proposed model has several benefits: (1) it takes into account the
similarity between users, which affects the probability of rumor spreading;
(2) it distinguishes between the RSS and OF, which are both important for the
rumor spreading; and (3) it provides a framework for understanding the rumor
spreading process.

Planetary nebulae (PNe) and HII regions provide a probe of the chemical
enrichment and star formation history of a galaxy from intermediate ages to the
present day. We present the first results of NTT spectroscopy of HII regions
and/or PNe in four nearby dwarf galaxies: Sextans A, Sextans B, NGC 3109, and
Fornax. For all PNe and some of the HII regions in these galaxies we have
obtained elemental abundances via the classic Te-method based on the detection
of the [OIII] 4363 line. The oxygen abundances in three HII regions of Sextans
A are all consistent within the individual rms uncertainties. The oxygen
abundance in the PN of Sextans A is however significantly higher. This PN is
even more enriched in nitrogen and helium, implying its classification as a PN
of Type I. The presumably unaffected PN abundances of S and Ar are well below
those in the HII regions, indicating a lower metallicity at the epoch of the PN
progenitor formation. For two HII regions in Sextans B, the oxygen abundances
do not differ within the rms uncertainties. The third one is, however, twice as
metal-rich, providing evidence for the inhomogeneity of the current metallicity
distribution in Sextans B. For the PN in Sextans B we measured an O/H that is
consistent with that of the low-metallicity HII regions. For NGC 3109 our
preliminary results indicate that the oxygen abundances of PNe and HII regions
are all within a small range of $\pm$0.15 dex. For the PN in Fornax, Ne, Ar and
S abundances suggest that the ISM metallicity was $\sim$0.3 dex lower at the
epoch of the PN progenitor's formation, compared to the O/H value derived for
the PN (abridged).

Planetary nebulae (PNe) and HII regions provide a probe of the chemical
enrichment and star formation history of a galaxy from intermediate ages to the
present day. We present the first results of NTT spectroscopy of HII regions
and/or PNe in four nearby dwarf galaxies:  Fornax, Leo I, Leo II and Leo III.
The spectra were reduced using IRAF and the final data sets contain 19 high
resolution spectra in the blue and 24 in the red. For each region, we find
that the metallicities are ranging from -1.15 to 0.05, and the star formation
rate at the time of the formation of the HII region is estimated to be in the
range of $0.01-2.0$ Mpc$^{3}$/yr. The highest metallicities are found in the
HII region in the Fornax dwarf spheroidal galaxy, which suggests that it
could be a pre-galactic or proto-galactic star forming region. The
lowest metallicities are found in the Leo II galaxy. The star formation rates
are found to be consistent with the expectation that PNe are long lived
objects that are much older than the HII regions. The star formation rates are
also consistent with the expectation that the PNe are a good sample of the
interstellar medium of the galaxies.

rates were estimated for each galaxy. The results show that the metallicities
range from -1.15 to 0.05, and the star formation rates are in the range of
$0.01-2.0$ Mpc$^{3}$/yr. The highest metallicities are found in Fornax, which
may indicate a pre-galactic or proto-galactic star forming region. The lowest
metallicities are found in Leo II. The star formation rates are consistent with
the expectation that PNe are long-lived objects and a good sample of the

The global structure of galaxy clusters and its evolution are tested within a
large set of TREESPH simulations, so to allow a fair statistical comparison
with available X-ray data. Structure tests are based on the "power ratios",
introduced by Buote & Tsai. Cosmological models considered are CDM, LCDM
(Omega_L=0.7) and CHDM (1 mass.neu., Omega_h = 0.2). All models are normalized
to provide a fair number density of clusters. For each model we run a P3M
simulation in a large box, where we select the most massive 40 clusters. Going
back to the initial redshift we run a hydro-TREESPH simulation for each of
them. In this way we perform a statistical comparison of the global morphology
of clusters, for each cosmological model, with ROSAT data, using Student
t-test, F-test and K-S test. The last test and its generalization to 2--D
distributions are also used to compare the joint distributions of 2 or 3 power
ratios. We find that, using DM distribution, instead of gas, as done by some
authors, leads to biased results, as baryons are distributed in a less
structured way than DM. We also find that the cosmological models considered
have different behaviours in these tests: LCDM has the worst performance. CDM
and our CHDM have similar scores. The general trend of power ratio
distributions is already fit by these models, but a further improvement is
expected either from a different DM mix or a non-flat CDM model.

The global structure of galaxy clusters and its evolution are tested within a
large set of TREESPH simulations, so to allow a fair statistical comparison
with available X-ray data. Structure tests are based on the "power ratios",
introduced by Buote & Tsai. Cosmological models considered are CDM, and
omega=0.7, 0.8, 0.9 and 1.0. The power ratios for the density profile
of the ICM are presented for the model omega=0.9. These ratios are
interpreted as a measure of the degree of clustering of ICM gas, and are
compared to the ratios obtained from available data (Einstein
Cross-correlation Function, X-ray flux power spectrum).  ICM densities are
calculated by integrating the mass distribution of the clusters.  ICM mass
fractions are compared with a Hubble constant of 50 km/s/Mpc and a
cosmological constant lambda=0, in order to test the evolution of ICM
mass fraction with redshift.

Abstract:
The structure of galaxy clusters and its evolution are studied within a large set of TREESPH simulations. The power ratios, introduced by Buote & Tsai, are used to test the global structure of galaxy clusters. We present the power ratios for the density profile of the ICM for the model omega=0.9 and compare them with the ratios obtained from available data. The ICM densities are calculated by integrating the mass distribution of the clusters. The ICM mass fractions are compared with a Hubble constant of 50 km/s/Mpc and a cosmological constant lambda=0, in order to test the evolution of ICM mass fraction with redshift. The results of the simulations and the comparison with available data provide a fair statistical basis for testing the evolution of galaxy clusters in the universe.

The TREESPH simulations used in this study provide a powerful tool for studying the global structure of galaxy clusters and its evolution. The power ratios, introduced by Buote & Tsai, are a useful measure of the degree of clustering of ICM gas, and the comparison with available data provides a fair statistical basis for testing the evolution of galaxy clusters in the universe. The results of the simulations and the comparison with available data provide a comprehensive understanding of the structure and evolution of galaxy clusters.

The study of galaxy clusters and their evolution is an active area of research, and this study contributes to our understanding of the global structure and evolution of galaxy clusters. The use of TREESPH simulations and the power ratios provides a powerful tool for studying the structure and evolution of galaxy clusters, and the comparison with available data provides a fair statistical basis for testing the evolution of galaxy clusters in the universe.

Our goal is to morphologically classify the sources identified in the images
of the J-PLUS early data release (EDR) into compact (stars) or extended
(galaxies) using a suited Bayesian classifier. J-PLUS sources exhibit two
distinct populations in the r-band magnitude vs. concentration plane,
corresponding to compact and extended sources. We modelled the two-population
distribution with a skewed Gaussian for compact objects and a log-normal
function for the extended ones. The derived model and the number density prior
based on J-PLUS EDR data were used to estimate the Bayesian probability of a
source to be star or galaxy. This procedure was applied pointing-by-pointing to
account for varying observing conditions and sky position. Finally, we combined
the morphological information from g, r, and i broad bands in order to improve
the classification of low signal-to-noise sources. The derived probabilities
are used to compute the pointing-by-pointing number counts of stars and
galaxies. The former increases as we approach to the Milky Way disk, and the
latter are similar across the probed area. The comparison with SDSS in the
common regions is satisfactory up to r ~ 21, with consistent numbers of stars
and galaxies, and consistent distributions in concentration and (g - i) colour
spaces. We implement a morphological star/galaxy classifier based on PDF
analysis, providing meaningful probabilities for J-PLUS sources to one
magnitude deeper (r ~ 21) than a classical boolean classification. These
probabilities are suited for the statistical study of 150k stars and 101k
galaxies with 15 < r < 21 present in the 31.7 deg2 of the J-PLUS EDR. In a
future version of the classifier, we will include J-PLUS colour information
from twelve photometric bands.

of the J-PLUS early data release (EDR) into compact (stars) or extended
(galaxies) using a suited Bayesian classifier. J-PLUS sources exhibit two
distinct populations in the r-band magnitude vs. concentration plane,
characterized by a clear separation into compact (stars) and extended
(galaxies) regions. For the classification of the sources into compact
(stars) or extended (galaxies) we used the following results of the
parameter estimation:
  1. A magnitude criterion for compact sources, i.e. r-band magnitude
     < 20.5, which separates compact (stars) from extended (galaxies).
  2. A shape parameter, β, which is a measure of the shape of the profile,
     with a lower β value indicating a more compact object. β < 1.2 for
     compact (stars) and β ≥ 1.2 for extended (galaxies).
  3. A size parameter, D25, which is a measure of the size of the object,
     with a smaller D25 value indicating a more compact object. D25 < 2.5 arcsec
     for compact (stars) and D25 ≥ 2.5 arcsec for extended (galaxies).
  4. A profile shape parameter, α, which measures the shape of the
     profile. α < 1.8 for compact (stars) and α ≥ 1.8 for extended
     (galaxies).
  5. A color parameter, m-r, which is a measure of the color of the
     object. m-r < 0.8 for compact (stars) and m-r ≥ 0.8 for extended
     (galaxies).
  The classification of a source into compact (stars) or extended
  (galaxies) based on these criteria is made by assigning a probability
  of membership to the two possible classes (compact (stars) or extended
  (galaxies)) and selecting the class with the highest probability as

We study both theoretically and experimentally switching dynamics in surface
stabilized ferroelectric liquid crystal cells with asymmetric boundary
conditions. In these cells the bounding surfaces are treated differently to
produce asymmetry in their anchoring properties. Our electro-optic measurements
of the switching voltage thresholds that are determined by the peaks of the
reversal polarization current reveal the frequency dependent shift of the
hysteresis loop. We examine the predictions of the uniform dynamical model with
the anchoring energy taken into account. It is found that the asymmetry effects
are dominated by the polar contribution to the anchoring energy. Frequency
dependence of the voltage thresholds is studied by analyzing the properties of
time-periodic solutions to the dynamical equation (cycles). For this purpose,
we apply the method that uses the parameterized half-period mappings for the
approximate model and relate the cycles to the fixed points of the composition
of two half-period mappings. The cycles are found to be unstable and can only
be formed when the driving frequency is lower than its critical value. The
polar anchoring parameter is estimated by making a comparison between the
results of modelling and the experimental data for the shift vs frequency
curve. For a double-well potential considered as a deformation of the
Rapini-Papoular potential, the branch of stable cycles emerges in the low
frequency region separated by the gap from the high frequency interval for
unstable cycles.

stabilized ferroelectric liquid crystal cells with asymmetric boundary
conditions. In these cells the bounding surfaces are treated differently to
produce asymmetry in their anchoring properties. Our electro-optic measurements
of the switching voltage thresholds that are determined by the director and
polarization configurations at specific field strengths will give us the
junction conditions and provide information about the interplay between
electrostriction, domain wall dynamics, and mechanical stresses that affect
the switching dynamics. The experimental setup allows us to manipulate the
symmetry of the boundary conditions while keeping other parameters constant,
so we can obtain information about the boundary effects on the switching
dynamics.  Our theoretical model is based on the Landau-De Gennes
free-energy functional, and is based on the assumption that the
electrostriction coefficient is not much smaller than the spontaneous strain
coefficient, leading to a strong interplay between the elastic and
electrostatic forces in the director field. The theoretical model predicts
that increasing the asymmetry of the boundary conditions can lead to
increasing the switching voltage thresholds and the distortion of the
director field.  This model will be compared with the experimental data to
gain insight into the switching dynamics of these cells.

with asymmetric boundary conditions.

by the director field, which is a measure of the polarization state in the
liquid crystal. In the case of surface stabilized ferroelectric liquid
crystals, the director field is also influenced by the boundary conditions,
which determine the anchoring properties of the liquid crystal at the
boundaries. These boundary conditions can be designed to be symmetric or
asymmetric, which can affect the switching dynamics of the liquid crystal.

crystals with asymmetric boundary conditions. We use a surface stabilized
ferroelectric liquid crystal cell with a gold electrode on one side and a
conducting polymer electrode on the other side. We manipulate the symmetry of
the boundary conditions by changing the orientation of the electrodes or the
magnitudes of the surface charges. We measure the switching voltage thresholds

The interest in understanding scaling limits of magnetic textures such as
domain walls spans the entire field of magnetism from its relativistic quantum
fundamentals to applications in information technologies. The traditional focus
of the field on ferromagnets has recently started to shift towards
antiferromagnets which offer a rich materials landscape and utility in
ultra-fast and neuromorphic devices insensitive to magnetic field
perturbations. Here we report the observation that domain walls in an epitaxial
crystal of antiferromagnetic CuMnAs can be atomically sharp. We reveal this
ultimate domain wall scaling limit using differential phase contrast imaging
within aberrationcorrected scanning transmission electron microscopy, which we
complement by X-ray magnetic dichroism microscopy and ab initio calculations.
We highlight that the atomically sharp domain walls are outside the remits of
established spin-Hamiltonian theories and can offer device functionalities
unparalleled in ferromagnets.

that we aim to address is to develop the necessary tools and techniques to
describe and predict the behavior of magnetic textures in the finite

to describe the complex behavior of magnetic textures, including the
introduction of disorder and the coupling with magnons and other excitations,

experimental data. This requires a multidisciplinary approach
involving materials science, statistical mechanics, and computational physics.

modeling tools to address the challenges outlined above. This includes:

  generated from the simulations, including machine learning methods.
* Developing new theoretical models that can capture the essential
  features of the magnetic textures in the finite temperature range.
* Developing new experimental methods to test and validate the new

We analyze a photon transport through an 1D open waveguide side coupled to
the $N$-photon microwave cavity with embedded artificial two- level atom
(qubit). The qubit state is probed by a weak signal at the fundamental
frequency of the waveguide. Within the formalism of projection operators and
non-Hermitian Hamiltonian approach we develop a one-photon approximation scheme
to obtain the photon wavefunction which allows for the calculation of the
probability amplitudes of the spontaneous transitions between the levels of two
Rabi doublets in $N$- photon cavity. We obtain analytic expressions for the
transmission and reflection factors of the microwave signal through a
waveguide, which contain the information of the qubit parameters. We show that
for small number of cavity photons the Mollow spectrum consists of four
spectral lines which is a direct manifestation of quantum nature of light. The
results obtained in the paper are of general nature and can be applied to any
type of qubits. The specific properties of the qubit are only encoded in the
two parameters: the energy $\Omega$ of the qubit and its coupling $\lambda$ to
the cavity photons.

We analyze a photon transport through an 1D open waveguide side coupled to
the $N$-photon microwave cavity with embedded artificial two- level atom
(qubit). The qubit state is probed by a weak signal at the fundamental
frequency of the waveguide. Within the formalism of quantum optics, we
analyze the steady-state properties of the open waveguide cavity system. The
cavity modes are described by the coupled master equation for the field
operator and the two-level atom qubit.

The system is governed by the master equation given by
\begin{align}
\frac{d\hat{E}}{dt} &= -i\Delta\hat{E}e^{i\omega_{c}t} - i\gamma_{\text{cav}}\hat{E} + i\gamma_{\text{C}}\hat{\sigma}_{\uparrow}\hat{E} - i\gamma_{\text{C}}\hat{\sigma}_{\downarrow}\hat{E}\\
\frac{d\hat{\sigma}_{\uparrow}}{dt} &= -i\delta\hat{\sigma}_{\uparrow}e^{i\omega_{c}t} - \left(\kappa_{\text{cav}} + \gamma_{\text{B}} + \gamma_{\text{C}}\right)\hat{\sigma}_{\uparrow}\\
\frac{d\hat{\sigma}_{\downarrow}}{dt} &= -i\delta\hat{\sigma}_{\downarrow}e^{i\omega_{c}t} + \left(\kappa_{\text{cav}} + \gamma_{\text{B}} + \gamma_{\cav}\right)\hat{\sigma}_{\downarrow}\\
\end{align}
with $\hat{E}$ being the electric field operator, $\hat{\sigma}_{\uparrow}$ and $\hat{\sigma}_{\downarrow}$ being the Pauli operators for the upper and lower energy level, respectively.

Our focus is on the steady-state of the open waveguide system by assuming $t \gg \tau_{\text{cav}}$, $\tau_{\text{B}}$, $\tau_{\text{C}}$, and $\tau_{\text{cav}}$ as the decay rates of the cavity, the bosonic field, the atomic state, and the cavity, respectively.

In this paper, we introduce a novel approach for ground plane normal
estimation of wheeled vehicles. In practice, the ground plane is dynamically
changed due to braking and unstable road surface. As a result, the vehicle
pose, especially the pitch angle, is oscillating from subtle to obvious. Thus,
estimating ground plane normal is meaningful since it can be encoded to improve
the robustness of various autonomous driving tasks (e.g., 3D object detection,
road surface reconstruction, and trajectory planning). Our proposed method only
uses odometry as input and estimates accurate ground plane normal vectors in
real time. Particularly, it fully utilizes the underlying connection between
the ego pose odometry (ego-motion) and its nearby ground plane. Built on that,
an Invariant Extended Kalman Filter (IEKF) is designed to estimate the normal
vector in the sensor's coordinate. Thus, our proposed method is simple yet
efficient and supports both camera- and inertial-based odometry algorithms. Its
usability and the marked improvement of robustness are validated through
multiple experiments on public datasets. For instance, we achieve
state-of-the-art accuracy on KITTI dataset with the estimated vector error of
0.39{\deg}. Our code is available at github.com/manymuch/ground_normal_filter.

In this paper, we introduce a novel approach for ground plane normal
estimation of wheeled vehicles. In practice, the ground plane is dynamically
changed due to braking and unstable road surface. As a result, the vehicle
pose, especially the pitch angle, is oscillating from subtle to obvious. Thus,
estimating the ground plane normal is a challenging task for ground vehicle
systems.

  The proposed approach uses a combination of three different sensors
providing complementary information: Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU), Gyro
Scope and LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging). The IMU provides the
sensors orientation and provides the orientation of the wheels, while the Gyro
Scope provides the angular velocity and the pitch angle of the wheels. The LiDAR
provides the distance from the vehicle to the ground plane. By integrating these
sensors data, we can estimate the ground plane normal, even when the vehicle
pose is oscillating.

  Our approach can be divided into three main steps: data fusion,
estimation of ground plane distance, and ground plane normal estimation. In the
data fusion step, we combine the data from the three sensors. In the estimation of
ground plane distance step, we use the distance data from the LiDAR to estimate
the distance from the vehicle to the ground plane. In the ground plane normal
estimation step, we use the data from the IMU and Gyro Scope to estimate the
ground plane normal.

  The results of our approach are validated using real-world data.
Comparing our approach with other state-of-the-art methods, we show that our
approach has a lower computational complexity and is more robust to noisy data.

  This paper contributes to the field of ground vehicle systems by providing a novel
approach for ground plane normal estimation. Our approach can be used in various
ground vehicle systems such as autonomous driving, robotic roadmapping, and
autonomous off-road vehicles.

The availability of large scale databases containing imaging and non-imaging
data, such as the UK Biobank, represents an opportunity to improve our
understanding of healthy and diseased bodily function. Cardiac motion atlases
provide a space of reference in which the motion fields of a cohort of subjects
can be directly compared. In this work, a cardiac motion atlas is built from
cine MR data from the UK Biobank (~ 6000 subjects). Two automated quality
control strategies are proposed to reject subjects with insufficient image
quality. Based on the atlas, three dimensionality reduction algorithms are
evaluated to learn data-driven cardiac motion descriptors, and statistical
methods used to study the association between these descriptors and non-imaging
data. Results show a positive correlation between the atlas motion descriptors
and body fat percentage, basal metabolic rate, hypertension, smoking status and
alcohol intake frequency. The proposed method outperforms the ability to
identify changes in cardiac function due to these known cardiovascular risk
factors compared to ejection fraction, the most commonly used descriptor of
cardiac function. In conclusion, this work represents a framework for further
investigation of the factors influencing cardiac health.

The availability of large scale databases containing imaging and non-imaging
data, such as the UK Biobank, represents an opportunity to improve our
understanding of healthy and diseased bodily function. Cardiac motion atlases
provide a space of reference in which the motion fields of a cohort of subjects
can be compared. Motion fields are the rate of change in the position of the
heart over time. For a motion atlas to be useful, the fields must be calculated
accurately and consistently. The development of automated motion field
calculations is a challenging task due to the variability in shape and motion
between individuals. One approach is to use the geometric constraints of the
cardiac model. These constraints define the possible range of motion and
position of the heart. However, simply applying these constraints does not
guarantee accurate calculations. It is possible to use the data from existing
motion atlases to improve the accuracy of the calculations. This is because the
motion fields calculated from these data can be used as prior knowledge to
influence the optimization of the model parameters.

In this paper we present an approach for the development of a cardiac motion
atlas using an optimization method based on the use of prior knowledge from
existing motion atlases. We use a 3D finite element model of the heart as the
cardiac model. The motion fields are calculated using a combination of the
data from the existing motion atlases and the optimization of the model
parameters. We use a numerical optimization method to minimize the difference
between the calculated motion fields and the data from the existing motion
atlases. This method is based on the use of a non-linear least squares
algorithm. The method is robust and efficient, and it can be used to
calculate the motion fields for a large number of individuals.

The benefits of using prior knowledge from existing motion atlases are that it
allows for the use of a small number of data points to constrain the motion fields
and improve the accuracy of the calculations. The motion fields calculated from
these data can be used to train a model that can be used to calculate the motion
fields for a large number of individuals. The use of prior knowledge also allows
for the development of a more robust and efficient optimization method.

The results of the present study show that the use of prior knowledge from
existing motion atlases can improve the accuracy of the motion field
calculations. The use of prior knowledge results in a more robust and efficient
optimization method. The results also show that the use of prior knowledge from
existing motion atlases can be used to calculate the motion fields for a large
number of individuals.

best model is by measuring the lens parameters, which can be obtained by
measuring the Einstein crossing time and the parallax effect on the

magnitude smaller than for models with dark halos. This can be used as a test

In recent years various models for the Galactic distribution of massive
compact halo objects (MACHOs) have been proposed for the interpretation of
microlensing toward the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). A direct way to fit the
best model is by measuring the lens parameters, which can be obtained by modeling
the mass of the lens and its properties. This requires the identification of
distant host galaxies of MACHOs, whose combined mass is less than $M_{\odot}$.
This identification is hard because in the LMC, stars and other objects are
clumped together in small groups, making it difficult to identify galaxies with
masses in the range 0.01-0.1 solar masses.  In order to improve the quality of
the lens parameter determination, we would like to explore alternative
approaches to identify distant host galaxies of MACHOs.

Here are some possible alternative approaches to identify distant host galaxies
of MACHOs:
-  **Infrared excess**: If the MACHO is a star, its infrared excess can be
    measured through the near-infrared observations. The infrared excess can be
    used to estimate the temperature and the mass of the star, thus identifying
    the host galaxy.
-  **X-ray emission**: MACHOs can emit X-rays if they are in contact with a
    circumstellar disk. The detection of X-ray emission can be used to estimate
    the mass of the MACHO, and thus identify the host galaxy.
-  **Gravitational lensing**: The gravitational lensing effect can be used to
    map the mass distribution of the lensing galaxy. The mass distribution can be
    compared to the mass distribution of the MACHO to determine if they are
    identical.
-  **Proper motion**: The proper motion of the MACHO can be measured by
    observing it over a long time period. The proper motion can be used to
    determine the mass of the MACHO, and thus identify the host galaxy.

In this project, we propose to explore these alternative approaches to identify
distant host galaxies of MACHOs. We will start by selecting a sample of MACHO
candidates in the LMC. We will then apply the alternative approaches to each
candidate and determine if the host galaxy can be identified. The project will
involve a combination of data analysis, astrophysical modeling, and simulation
tools. We will use publicly available data from existing surveys and missions,
such as the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, the Spitzer Space Telescope, and the
Chandra X-ray Observatory.

Vertebral body compression fractures are reliable early signs of
osteoporosis. Though these fractures are visible on Computed Tomography (CT)
images, they are frequently missed by radiologists in clinical settings. Prior
research on automatic methods of vertebral fracture classification proves its
reliable quality; however, existing methods provide hard-to-interpret outputs
and sometimes fail to process cases with severe abnormalities such as highly
pathological vertebrae or scoliosis. We propose a new two-step algorithm to
localize the vertebral column in 3D CT images and then to simultaneously detect
individual vertebrae and quantify fractures in 2D. We train neural networks for
both steps using a simple 6-keypoints based annotation scheme, which
corresponds precisely to current medical recommendation. Our algorithm has no
exclusion criteria, processes 3D CT in 2 seconds on a single GPU, and provides
an intuitive and verifiable output. The method approaches expert-level
performance and demonstrates state-of-the-art results in vertebrae 3D
localization (the average error is 1 mm), vertebrae 2D detection (precision is
0.99, recall is 1), and fracture identification (ROC AUC at the patient level
is 0.93).

osteoporosis. Though these fractures are visible on Computed Tomography (CT)
images, they are frequently missed by radiologists in clinical settings. Prior
research on automatic methods of vertebral fracture classification proves its
reliable quality; however, existing methods typically require manual
annotation and additional features extraction for high-quality results. In this
work, we proposed a novel fully-automated approach using Convolutional Neural
Networks (CNNs) to classify vertebral compression fractures and provide
instantaneous feedback on the detection results. Our approach has been tested
on a large-scale dataset of CT scans of 230 patients, with results showing

of 97.9%. The approach has been validated on both a small-scale dataset and
a large-scale dataset, demonstrating its robustness and reliability. The proposed
model provides a valuable contribution to the field of medical image analysis and
has the potential to revolutionize the diagnosis and treatment of vertebral

Glaucoma is a major eye disease, leading to vision loss in the absence of
proper medical treatment. Current diagnosis of glaucoma is performed by
ophthalmologists who are often analyzing several types of medical images
generated by different types of medical equipment. Capturing and analyzing
these medical images is labor-intensive and expensive. In this paper, we
present a novel computational approach towards glaucoma diagnosis and
localization, only making use of eye fundus images that are analyzed by
state-of-the-art deep learning techniques. Specifically, our approach leverages
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Gradient-weighted Class Activation
Mapping (Grad-CAM) for glaucoma diagnosis and localization, respectively.
Quantitative and qualitative results, as obtained for a small-sized dataset
with no segmentation ground truth, demonstrate that the proposed approach is
promising, for instance achieving an accuracy of 0.91$\pm0.02$ and an ROC-AUC
score of 0.94 for the diagnosis task. Furthermore, we present a publicly
available prototype web application that integrates our predictive model, with
the goal of making effective glaucoma diagnosis available to a wide audience.

proper medical treatment. Current diagnosis of glaucoma is performed by
ophthalmologists who are often analyzing several types of medical images
generated by different types of medical equipment. Capturing and analyzing
these medical images is labor-intensive and time-consuming, which may lead
to a delayed diagnosis of the disease. 

In this paper, we propose a deep learning-based approach for the
classification of glaucoma from medical images. We utilized a convolutional
neural network (CNN) with transfer learning, which is able to learn features
from pre-trained networks and adapt them to our specific medical images. Our
approach utilizes a data augmentation technique to increase the size of the
training dataset, which is essential for training a CNN. We also employed
a multi-class classification algorithm to handle the presence of different
types of medical images. The proposed approach was evaluated on a publicly
available dataset, and our results show that our approach is able to
achieve a high accuracy in classification of glaucoma from medical images.

We also proposed a method to automatically generate a glaucoma diagnosis
report. This method uses a text generator to generate a diagnosis report
based on the predicted labels from the CNN. The report includes information
such as the type of glaucoma, the stage of the disease, and the
recommendations for further treatment. The proposed report generation
method was also evaluated on the same publicly available dataset and
showed promising results.

Our approach has the potential to improve the diagnosis of glaucoma from
medical images, and to automate the process of generating a diagnosis
report. This can lead to a faster and more accurate diagnosis of the disease,
which can ultimately improve patient outcomes.  To further improve the
approach, we plan to explore the use of transfer learning from other medical
domains and to incorporate additional data sources such as genomics and
electroencephalography (EEG) data. 

This paper presents a new approach to the classification of glaucoma from
medical images using deep learning techniques. Our approach achieves a high
accuracy in classification of glaucoma from medical images and provides a
method for automatically generating a diagnosis report. We believe that our
approach has the potential to improve the diagnosis of glaucoma from medical
images and to automate the process of generating a diagnosis report, which
can ultimately improve patient outcomes.

Graph neural network (GNN) and label propagation algorithm (LPA) are both
message passing algorithms, which have achieved superior performance in
semi-supervised classification. GNN performs feature propagation by a neural
network to make predictions, while LPA uses label propagation across graph
adjacency matrix to get results. However, there is still no effective way to
directly combine these two kinds of algorithms. To address this issue, we
propose a novel Unified Message Passaging Model (UniMP) that can incorporate
feature and label propagation at both training and inference time. First, UniMP
adopts a Graph Transformer network, taking feature embedding and label
embedding as input information for propagation. Second, to train the network
without overfitting in self-loop input label information, UniMP introduces a
masked label prediction strategy, in which some percentage of input label
information are masked at random, and then predicted. UniMP conceptually
unifies feature propagation and label propagation and is empirically powerful.
It obtains new state-of-the-art semi-supervised classification results in Open
Graph Benchmark (OGB).

isotropic random geometric graph (IRGG) has been developed and its topological
properties in two dimensions have been studied in details. The defining
characteristics of RGG and IRGG are the same --- two nodes are connected by an
edge if their distance is less than a fixed value, called the connection
radius. However, IRGGs have two major differences from regular RGGs. Firstly,
the shape of their boundaries --- which is circular. It brings very little
changes in final results but gives a significant advantage in analytical
calculations of the network properties. Secondly, it opens up the possibility
of an empty concentric region inside the network. The empty region contains no
nodes but allows the communicating edges between the nodes to pass through it.
This second difference causes significant alterations in physically relevant
network properties such as average degree, connectivity, clustering coefficient
and average shortest path. Analytical expressions for most of these features
have been provided. These results agree well with those obtained from
simulations. Apart from the applicability of the model due to its symmetry and
simplicity, the scope of incorporating a penetrable cavity makes it suitable
for potential applications in wireless communication networks that often have a
node-free region.

properties in two dimensions have been studied in details. The defining
characteristics of RGG and IRGG are the same --- two nodes are connected by
an edge if the distance between them is less than a certain scale parameter.
However, the main difference between the two models is the isotropic
property in IRGG. The nodes of the IRGG have a uniform distribution of angles
relative to their positions on the plane. This results in a different probability
distribution of edge lengths compared to the RGG.

studied in the two extreme cases: a large number of nodes with a small
scale parameter and a small number of nodes with a large scale parameter.
  The connectivity of IRGG is studied using the random graph theory,
where the probability of an edge between two nodes is calculated based on the
angle between the nodes and the scale parameter. This method allows us to
calculate the probability of an edge between two nodes and find the number of
connected components and the size of the giant component in the IRGG.

RGG. The results show that the IRGG has a different topological structure
compared to the RGG. The giant component in the IRGG is smaller than that of

graphs and their applications in modeling real-world networks. The results of this
paper can be used to analyze and understand the behavior of networks in
different scenarios, such as network communication, transportation, and social
